sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 10

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 10:1
items.html:
All the substance ascribed to the emanated is the desire to receive; whatever it has beyond this is ascribed to the Emanator.
And know: the discernment of the desire to receive in the emanated — which has been well clarified to be the vessel in it — know that it is also the entire general substance ascribed to the emanated, such that all being apart from it is ascribed to the Emanator.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 10:2
items.html:
The desire to receive is the first form of every essence, and the first form we define by the name "substance," because we have no attainment in the essence itself.
Although the discernment of the "desire to receive" seems, at first sight, an incident and a form within the essence — how then do we take it for the substance of the essence? — yet so it is also with the essences near to us: it is our way to call the first form of an essence by the name of the first substance of that essence, because we have no attainment or grasp whatsoever of any substance. For all our five senses are unfit for this: sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch offer to the contemplative intellect mere forms of the incidents of the essence, depicted through the cooperation of our senses. For example, even if we take the tiny microscopic atoms in the first elements of some essence, separated out by the craft of chemistry — they too are mere forms that so depict themselves to the eyes; or, more precisely, they are recognized and discerned by us through the ways of the "desire to receive and to be received" that we find in them, by whose lawful operations it is possible to discern and to isolate those atoms into their kinds, down to the discernment of the first substance of that essence. Yet even then, these are only forces within the essence, not substance. And so you find that even in corporeality we have no other way of understanding the first substance except by positing that the first form is the first substance, bearing all the other incidents and forms that come after it — and all the more so in the upper worlds, where the tangible and the imagined do not apply.
